column1: According to Le Chatelier's principle, what happens when a stress is applied to a system in dynamic equilibrium?
column2: The equilibrium adjusts to minimize the effect of the stress.

column1: What is the effect on the equilibrium position when the concentration of a reactant is increased?
column2: The equilibrium shifts toward the right (products).

column1: How does an equilibrium system respond to the removal of a product?
column2: It shifts toward the right to generate more product.

column1: What is the result of increasing the concentration of a product in an equilibrium mixture?
column2: The equilibrium shifts toward the left (reactants).

column1: In which direction does the equilibrium shift if the concentration of a reactant is decreased?
column2: It shifts toward the left (the side of the decreased reactant).

column1: How does a decrease in container volume affect a gaseous equilibrium system?
column2: It shifts toward the side with the smaller number of gas molecules.

column1: What effect does an increase in container volume have on a gaseous equilibrium?
column2: It shifts toward the side with the larger number of gas molecules.

column1: If a gaseous reaction has the same number of molecules on both sides, what is the effect of changing the volume?
column2: Changing the volume has no effect on the equilibrium position.

column1: Under Le Chatelier's principle, why does a system shift toward fewer molecules when compressed?
column2: Reducing the number of molecules helps to minimize the resulting increase in pressure.

column1: How does raising the temperature affect the equilibrium position of an exothermic reaction?
column2: The equilibrium shifts toward the left (reactants).

column1: How does raising the temperature affect the equilibrium position of an endothermic reaction?
column2: The equilibrium shifts toward the right (products).

column1: In an exothermic reaction, heat can be treated as a _____.
column2: Product

column1: In an endothermic reaction, heat can be treated as a _____.
column2: Reactant

column1: What is the effect of lowering the temperature on the equilibrium of an exothermic reaction?
column2: The equilibrium shifts toward the right (products).

column1: What is the effect of lowering the temperature on the equilibrium of an endothermic reaction?
column2: The equilibrium shifts toward the left (reactants).

column1: Which specific factor is the only one capable of changing the numerical value of the equilibrium constant $K$?
column2: Temperature

column1: What is the effect of a catalyst on the position of a chemical equilibrium?
column2: A catalyst does not shift the equilibrium position.

column1: If a catalyst does not shift equilibrium, what is its primary function in a reversible reaction?
column2: It increases the speed at which equilibrium is reached.

column1: How does a catalyst influence the forward and reverse reaction rates?
column2: It speeds up both the forward and the reverse reactions to the same extent.

column1: Why does a catalyst have no effect on the value of the equilibrium constant $K$?
column2: It does not change the standard Gibbs free energy of reaction ($\Delta G_r^\circ$).

column1: What happens to the equilibrium composition if an inert gas is added at constant volume?
column2: The equilibrium composition remains unaffected.

column1: Why does adding an inert gas at constant volume fail to shift the equilibrium?
column2: The partial pressures and molar concentrations of the reacting gases remain unchanged.

column1: The decrease in solubility of a salt caused by adding an ion already present in the solution is called the _____.
column2: Common-ion effect

column1: How does adding $OH^-$ ions to a saturated solution of a heavy metal hydroxide affect its solubility?
column2: It reduces the solubility, causing more hydroxide to precipitate.

column1: In the Haber process, $N_2(g) + 3 H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 NH_3(g) + \text{heat}$, what pressure condition favors ammonia production?
column2: High pressure favors the side with fewer gas molecules (ammonia).

column1: In the Haber process, $N_2(g) + 3 H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 NH_3(g) + \text{heat}$, how does cooling the system affect the yield?
column2: Cooling favors the product because the reaction is exothermic.

column1: For an endothermic reaction, does the equilibrium constant $K$ increase or decrease as temperature rises?
column2: It increases.

column1: For an exothermic reaction, does the equilibrium constant $K$ increase or decrease as temperature rises?
column2: It decreases.

column1: What equation quantitatively relates the change in the equilibrium constant to a change in temperature?
column2: The van 't Hoff equation

column1: Concept: Dynamic Equilibrium
column2: Definition: A state where forward and reverse processes continue at matching rates, resulting in no net change in composition.

column1: What is the spontaneous direction of change for a reaction mixture with $Q < K$ at constant temperature and pressure?
column2: The forward direction (toward more products).

column1: What is the spontaneous direction of change for a reaction mixture with $Q > K$ at constant temperature and pressure?
column2: The reverse direction (toward more reactants).

column1: In a reaction mixture at equilibrium, what is the value of the Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G$)?
column2: Zero ($\Delta G = 0$).

column1: How does the activation energy of the forward reaction compare to the reverse in an endothermic process?
column2: The activation energy is higher for the forward direction than for the reverse direction.

column1: Why is the forward rate of an endothermic reaction more sensitive to temperature increases than the reverse rate?
column2: Because the forward reaction has a higher activation energy.

column1: Why is the reverse rate of an exothermic reaction more sensitive to temperature increases than the forward rate?
column2: Because the reverse reaction has a higher activation energy in an exothermic process.

column1: What happens to the amount of $I_2(g)$ in the equilibrium $I_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 I(g)$ when the system is expanded?
column2: The amount of $I_2$ decreases as the equilibrium shifts toward more gas molecules.

column1: How does adding a solid reactant or product affect the position of a chemical equilibrium?
column2: Adding a solid typically has no effect on the equilibrium position.

column1: If $\Delta H_r^\circ$ is negative, how will the ratio $K_2/K_1$ behave if $T_2 > T_1$?
column2: The ratio will be less than 1 ($K$ decreases).

column1: If $\Delta H_r^\circ$ is positive, how will the ratio $K_2/K_1$ behave if $T_2 > T_1$?
column2: The ratio will be greater than 1 ($K$ increases).

column1: In the context of Le Chatelier's principle, 'stress' refers to a change in concentration, pressure, volume, or _____.
column2: Temperature

column1: Does a catalyst change the energies of the reactants or products?
column2: No, it only lowers the activation energy by providing a different pathway.

column1: In a system of only gases, why does decreasing volume shift equilibrium to the side with fewer moles?
column2: To reduce the number of gas molecules and counteract the increased pressure.

column1: How does the presence of an iron catalyst affect the equilibrium yield of ammonia in the industrial Haber process?
column2: It has no effect on the equilibrium yield; it only speeds up the reaction.

column1: For the equilibrium $2 SO_2(g) + O_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 SO_3(g) + 198 \text{ kJ}$, what happens if heat is added?
column2: The equilibrium shifts to the left (reactants).

column1: For the equilibrium $H_2(g) + I_2(g) + 51.9 \text{ kJ} \rightleftharpoons 2 HI(g)$, what happens if the temperature is increased?
column2: The equilibrium shifts to the right (products).

column1: Why do industrial processes often operate far from equilibrium?
column2: By continuously removing products, the reaction is forced to continue generating more substance.

column1: What happens to the concentration of $Cl^-(aq)$ if $NaCl(s)$ is added to a saturated solution of $NaCl$?
column2: It remains unchanged because the solution is already saturated and at equilibrium with the solid.

column1: If a system at equilibrium has $\Delta G_r^\circ > 0$, are reactants or products favored?
column2: Reactants are favored ($K < 1$).

column1: If a system at equilibrium has $\Delta G_r^\circ < 0$, are reactants or products favored?
column2: Products are favored ($K > 1$).

column1: In the equilibrium $CaCO_3(s) \rightleftharpoons CaO(s) + CO_2(g)$, how does adding more $CO_2(g)$ affect the amount of $CaCO_3(s)$?
column2: The amount of $CaCO_3(s)$ increases as the equilibrium shifts to the left.

column1: What is the effect of increasing the concentration of $SCN^-$ in the equilibrium $Fe^{3+}(aq) + SCN^-(aq) \rightleftharpoons Fe(SCN)^{2+}(aq)$?
column2: The equilibrium shifts to the right, increasing the concentration of the red complex $Fe(SCN)^{2+}$.

column1: How does adding $Ag^+$ to a solution of $Fe(SCN)^{2+}$ affect the color, given $Ag^+$ precipitates $SCN^-$?
column2: The red color lightens as the equilibrium shifts left to replace removed $SCN^-$.

column1: In an aqueous equilibrium, does adding pure liquid water usually shift the equilibrium?
column2: It can shift the equilibrium if it changes the molar concentrations of the solutes.

column1: When considering the effect of volume on equilibrium, why are solids and liquids often ignored?
column2: Because their concentrations/densities are nearly independent of pressure and change very little with volume.

column1: According to the Boltzmann distribution, what factor in the rate constant formula is most sensitive to temperature change?
column2: The exponential factor $e^{-E_{min}/RT}$.

column1: If a reaction is exothermic, will the reverse activation energy be higher or lower than the forward activation energy?
column2: The reverse activation energy will be higher.

column1: For the equilibrium $CO(g) + H_2O(g) \rightleftharpoons CO_2(g) + H_2(g)$, how does increasing the partial pressure of $CO_2$ affect $H_2$?
column2: The partial pressure of $H_2$ will decrease.

column1: For the equilibrium $PCl_5(g) \rightleftharpoons PCl_3(g) + Cl_2(g)$, how does decreasing the volume affect the amount of $PCl_5$?
column2: The amount of $PCl_5$ increases.

column1: What is the relationship between the equilibrium constant $K$ and the rate constants of the forward ($k_f$) and reverse ($k_r$) reactions?
column2: $K = k_f / k_r$
